Jahaan Kanji Investigative  ·  Animal Welfare  ·  Ontario
Investigative Report  ·  March 2026

She Has Never Been
Called By a Name

By Jahaan Kanji  ·  March 2026
Scroll

This story was supposed to be about shelters. I started where most people start: at the end of the pipeline, in the kennels, with intake numbers, euthanasia stats, and dogs waiting behind glass. It wasn't enough. The further I reported, the further back the story kept pulling me — past the shelters, past the surrender forms, past the phone trees and the eleven-minute hold music, all the way back to a barn in Wellington County, a wire cage, a dog who has never been called by a name, and the listing designed to make sure you never think about her.

This is a story about an industry. A calculated, invisible, generationally entrenched industry that operates in plain sight — behind emoji-laden listings, meaningless registration certificates, and the careful distance between a clean room where puppies are shown and a barn where their mothers live and die on wire floors. An industry that shoots or drowns its females when they stop producing and fills the cage before the week is out. An industry propped up by a government that has legislated against it in language while quietly refusing to build the mechanisms that would make enforcement possible.

The government bears responsibility for this failure. By the time you finish reading, I think you'll find — the way I found — that it doesn't stand alone. It has company. And some of that company is us.

When I decided to report on shelter overcrowding in Toronto, my first instinct was straightforward: call the shelters. The first didn't answer. The second put me on hold for eleven minutes before a recording told me to try again during business hours. The third connected me to an interactive voice response system with the cheerful neutrality of a bank's customer service line.

Phone tree — Toronto area shelter, 2026

"If you're looking to surrender your pet, press 1. If surrendering is not an option, for certain cases, we may be able to provide euthanasia assistance, press 2. If you're looking for a pet to be euthanized, press 3."

There were other options. Adoption was in there somewhere. So was volunteering. The euthanasia option exists for animals who are critically ill, elderly, or whose behaviour poses a danger to themselves or others — but hearing it offered in the same mechanical sequence, in the same pleasant tone, was what stopped me. It was that they'd been distilled into a phone tree. Surrender your pet. Or, if that feels like too much paperwork, we can help with the other thing. Press 2. Press 3. The same mechanical voice, the same pleasant tone, all the way down.

I called seven shelters trying to arrange an observational visit. One told me I could watch the dogs, but only through a window. The reasoning stopped me: when someone came in and left without taking a dog, it caused measurable distress. The dogs would see a person. They would hope. They would watch that person leave. Better to keep the glass between us — to let them be observed without triggering the specific cruelty of almost.

I chose not to go. I couldn't bear the thought of standing at that window and then walking back to my car. It's possible that was the wrong call for a journalist. But I'm not sure it was the wrong call for a person, and I haven't fully resolved the distance between those two things.

What I was being kept from seeing was the end of a pipeline that begins somewhere most people never think to look — not at the shelters, but in the legislation that was supposed to prevent all of this, and the industry that legislation has quietly failed to touch.

Part I

The Law That Made Them Laugh

The press release landed in December 2023 with the kind of language governments reach for when they want to sound decisive without committing to anything specific: Ontario was banning puppy mills.11 The Preventing Unethical Puppy Sales Act — the PUPS Act, a name that almost parodies itself — would formalize what qualified as a puppy mill and impose stricter controls over breeding operations across the province, stacking onto the existing Provincial Animal Welfare Services framework that advocates had already spent years criticizing as toothless.1 On paper, the legislation targeted the visible horrors: dogs so matted their fur had fused to their skin, animals standing in their own waste, females bred before their first birthday, puppies pulled from their mothers before fifty-six days had passed.2 Anyone who supplied breeding animals, leased space, or helped facilitate sales could face charges, with every violation prosecuted as a separate offence.3 It was, by the standards of Ontario animal welfare legislation, ambitious.

The people closest to the problem were not impressed.

Lorraine Houston has been running Speaking of Dogs Rescue for twenty-five years. She told me what happened in the Mennonite communities west of Highway 400 when the PUPS Act first came out. A woman she works with — someone who has spent years quietly earning the trust of operators in the Tri-Cities area — told Houston what she overheard one afternoon: the Mennonites were laughing.

"They said, we can just get grandfathered, and nobody's going to come anyway. They were basically saying that the act is a joke. It won't affect us at all, don't worry. None of us really have a lot of faith in it."4

Lorraine Houston — Speaking of Dogs Rescue, 25 years

It is worth stopping here to understand what exactly they were laughing at. Because the operations running west of Highway 400 are not small. They are not accidental. They are multigenerational agricultural enterprises structured by the same logic as any livestock operation: maximize output, minimize cost, dispose of what stops producing. Breeding females are forced to produce two litters a year, and the conditions are so poor that many have a lifespan of only a few years.5 There are no national laws prohibiting any of it. The PUPS Act contains no licensing mechanism, no registry, and no way to find these operations unless someone calls in a complaint. And the operations are very good at not being found.

Investigators describe barns where barking hurts your ears, and ammonia stings your eyes — dogs packed in wire cages over piled feces, some so matted their breeds are unrecognizable.5 These are the conditions operating behind the pastoral exterior of Wellington County, the Region of Waterloo, Dufferin, Grey, Bruce, Huron, and Perth — invisible to the 100 inspectors responsible for a province larger than France and Spain combined. And some operations inhumanely de-bark dogs so they won't be discovered at all.7

Drew Woodley, Director of Government Relations at the Ontario SPCA and Humane Society, would not use the word joke. He is careful, institutional, precise in the way that people who have spent careers choosing words in public are precise. But when he described the government's regulatory proposal to me, the careful language arrived at the same place. "There's nothing in it that we as an organization were concerned about," he said.7 "It was really more, because it focused on a lot of record keeping, it was what wasn't in the package that we want to see addressed." Care standards. Health requirements. Licensing. The mechanisms that would make enforcement possible — the ones seven advocacy organizations told the government it needed — were absent. "Since that regulatory consultation process wrapped up," Woodley told me, "to the best of our knowledge, it hasn't moved forward."7

Camille Labchuk, a lawyer and the Executive Director of Animal Justice — the organization that testified against the PUPS Act at the Ontario legislature — puts it without Woodley's institutional restraint. "The PUPS Act, on its face, prohibits a puppy mill," she told me. "But then it defines a puppy mill very, very narrowly and not in any terms that would affect the actual operation of puppy mills that are currently in existence."8 She testified the same day as the Solicitor General. She watched him get asked, repeatedly, how many puppy mills were operating in Ontario. He had no answer. Not a rough estimate. Not a range. No idea. "The reason he has no answer," Labchuk told me, "is because there's no licensing or registration and there's no monitoring and simply no data on these operations."8 The law prohibits something it has no way of finding. The minister responsible for enforcing it cannot tell you how many of them exist. They knew. They have always known. And they did nothing.

One woman heard them laughing. Another spent a year waiting for regulations that never came. A third testified at Queen’s Park and watched the government nod along and do it anyway. The distance between those three experiences is the PUPS Act’s entire legacy.

Then came the penalties, which looked substantial until you asked the obvious question: substantial compared to what? A conviction carries a minimum $10,000 fine, jumping to $25,000 if a dog dies.10 Labchuk had even watched that minimum undercut in practice — a breeder convicted under the Act in the Chatham-Kent area received a sentence below the mandatory minimum, the plea arrangement quietly erasing the floor the legislation was supposed to set.8 The legislation created the architecture for future rules — breeder records, sales regulations, buyer protections — while leaving every one contingent on follow-up government action that has not materialized. Nearly two years after the Act’s passage, those regulations remain unfinished.12

The enforcement infrastructure tells the real story. Woodley named the catch-22 plainly: "You can't just show up at people's doors unannounced for no reason."7 Without a licensing registry, there is no legal basis for routine inspection. Without inspection, unlicensed operations stay invisible. Labchuk is precise about what this means legally: even when inspectors do find a mill, they can only act animal by animal. "They can generally only seize or take action in relation to that specific animal where they've identified that specific problem," she told me.8 "They would have no way of shutting down the entire operation. There's just no legal mechanism to do that." What Woodley calls a catch-22, Labchuk calls a structural failure — and she is more specific about where the failure lives: "The only way that a puppy mill could possibly be shut down — there's simply no mechanism in the law to shut one down right now, unless the enforcement agency lays a charge, the charge goes to trial, a court convicts, and then the court can include a prohibition order. And that would be at the very end of a years-long legal process. To my knowledge, that has never happened."8

Houston does not use either of their careful languages when she describes what that gap costs. She told me about the accusation levelled at rescues like hers — that by accepting dogs from mills, they become enablers. She understands the argument. She rejects it. “If we push back,” she told me, “they’re going to get rid of them. They don’t keep dogs that aren’t useful to them anymore.”4 Which is why, even when rescues are full, she can’t say no. She finds a way. You manage the flow as best you can. You keep going. You try not to think too hard about what happens to the ones you couldn’t reach.

I asked Houston what happens to dogs the mills are done with. She didn't hesitate. "They're a commodity, like stock on the shelf," she said. "Like a tomato that's shriveled up. When the dogs are too old and don't produce enough puppies, they are no longer useful or profitable.”4

Houston told me she had been in a meeting with a young lawyer that wanted to work in animal welfare. "She couldn't quite wrap her head around it – the fact these puppy mills treat the dogs in their care more like merchandise. When they don't want them anymore, they take them off the shelf." Her voice held the particular exhaustion of explaining a world that shouldn't need explaining.4 And when the stock piles up — when shelters are full, and rescues are full, and the transfer network has run out of room — Houston described what happens with the quiet precision of someone who has watched it many times. "We get surrender requests every day of the week. Whether it's an owner, whether it's a shelter, whether it's the indigenous communities, or from our puppy mill point person — everybody needs help. The number of dogs is so out of reach that none of us are going to be able to take on that number."4 Rescues reach out to their networks. Networks reach out to their partners. And sometimes, at the end of all of that reaching, there is nowhere left to go.

None of the people in these rooms — not the lawyers, not the rescuers, not the advocates who have spent fifteen years trying to be heard — can see the whole thing from where they stand. They each hold one edge of it.

What none of them can fully see from where they sit is the place where the pipeline actually starts. Not in a shelter intake form. Not in a Kijiji listing. In a barn. To understand what the PUPS Act failed to reach, and why the people inside those barns knew it before the ink was dry, you need to go to the barns.

0
puppy mill registries
in Ontario9
400+
estimated mills in
Wellington corridor alone13
2+yrs
regulations promised
still unwritten12
Part II

The Barn

Donna Power has spent fifteen years driving the roads that run west through the Region of Waterloo, Wellington County, Dufferin, Grey, Bruce, Huron, and Perth. She knows what the farms look like from the outside — the hand-painted vegetable stands, the horse-drawn buggies, the particular quiet of communities that have operated outside mainstream Ontario's economy for generations. She also knows what they look like from the inside, which is knowledge most people never acquire, because the inside is the part these operations have spent generations making sure you never see. Her spreadsheet lists over 200 properties in the Waterloo area alone. She estimates more than 400 mills in that corridor.13 "It's puppy mill central," she told me. "And most of them are Mennonite and Amish."13

This is the part of the story that makes people uncomfortable to say out loud — not because it isn't true, but because that discomfort has functioned, for decades, as a more effective shield than any law. "As soon as you say the word Mennonite," Power told me, "someone says religious persecution. That's how they stay invisible."13

She is careful to say what she means: this is not about faith. It is about an agricultural tradition, generations deep, that has categorized dogs the same way it categorizes pigs or chickens — as livestock, as crop, as revenue per unit. Puppies are just another harvest.

And when the harvest stops being profitable, the animal that produced it stops being worth the feed. Houston called them merchandise. Power goes further: "To them, the dog is just another crop."13 Two women, two decades of experience, the same word dressed in a different language.

To these operations, a breeding female is not a dog. She is inventory.

She has never been inside a house. She doesn't know what carpet feels like under her paws, or what it means when a hand reaches down slowly, without urgency, just to touch. She was born in a wire cage in a barn in Wellington County, and she has lived in that cage — or ones exactly like it — for every moment of her four years. The cage is roughly the length of her body plus six inches. There is a wire floor. There is a water bottle mounted to the side, the kind made for rabbits, and she has learned to drink from it with a tongue that was never meant to work that way. Her teeth have been rotting since her second year because no one has ever brushed them, and the water bottle means she can't lap properly to flush her mouth. It doesn't matter. Her teeth are not what she's here for.

What she is here for has happened five times now. Each time, something changes in her body — a heaviness, a need to nest that the wire floor makes impossible — and then there are puppies, and then the puppies are gone, sometimes before she's ready, sometimes before they are. She has never seen where they go. She has stopped trying to understand it.

She is four years old, and her body is beginning to show what four years of this costs. Her last litter was smaller. The one before that, smaller still. There are signs the people here have learned to read — output per cycle, size of litters, the diminishing return on a body run like a machine for its entire conscious life. She doesn't know she is being evaluated. She only knows that the mornings feel harder lately, that her hips ache in a way they didn't before, that the barn is very cold in February, and the wire floor holds the cold like it was built to.

She is not going to retire. There is no such thing as retirement here. When they come for her — and they will come, soon, because the math has already been done — it will not be to take her somewhere better. And somewhere on Kijiji, the puppies she produced six weeks ago are being described as “family raised” and “home-socialized,” and someone is already typing in their credit card number, already telling themselves that the $1,400 price tag means the breeder cares. It doesn’t.

She Has Never Been Called By a Name

And she is running out of time, and the government of Ontario knows this is happening and has decided, again, that it can wait.

Power describes what comes next with the flat affect of someone who has said it enough times that the horror has been compressed into fact. When a breeding female's output declines past the point of profitability, she is drowned, shot, or killed by choking or blunt force. Humane euthanasia costs money and requires a veterinarian, and the economics of these operations have never included either. "To them," Power told me, "the dog is just another crop. When it stops producing, you get rid of it. Same as you'd do with anything else on a farm that isn't earning its keep."13 There is no ceremony. No acknowledgment that anything with a heartbeat just ended. Just a body disposed of in the cheapest way available, and a cage that will be filled again before the week is out.

The government knows this is happening. It is not a secret. Power has been submitting documented evidence — photographs, video, addresses, spreadsheets — to animal welfare enforcement agencies for fifteen years. She has filed formal complaints with both the OSPCA and Animal Welfare Services. She has received nothing back from either. Not a confirmation of a visit. Not an outcome. Not an acknowledgment that anyone went. "You call in," she told me. "You get a ticket number. And then you never hear anything again."13

Before I ever sat down with Power, I had submitted FOI request #2026-00325 to the City of Toronto — a formal ask for surrender records broken down by reason, outcome data showing what happened to each animal, and transfer logs identifying where dogs went after Toronto Animal Services. Weeks later, the city wrote back to say the request would be delayed due to the volume of content involved. I told Power about it during our interview. She didn't pause. It'll be extended, she said, and extended again, and whatever eventually arrives will be redacted past the point of usefulness. "They hide behind the Privacy Act."13 Then, on March 4, 2026, a second letter arrived: a fee estimate. Forty-five hours of search time at $30 an hour. Total estimated cost: $1,350. Required deposit before they would begin: $675, payable by cheque or money order, mailed to City Hall. No credit cards. No debit. And depending on the volume of what the search turned up, additional fees might follow. I closed the request. Power had already told me what I would get for that money: redacted documents, missing data, a system so structurally incapable of accounting for itself that the absence of information isn't an accident. It's the design. The city charges $675 to confirm what Power has been saying for fifteen years.

Photo: Donna Power. A breeding female with her litter inside one of over 400 estimated unlicensed operations in Ontario's Wellington–Waterloo corridor. Power has been documenting these facilities for fifteen years.

Photo: Donna Power. Dogs from unlicensed breeders in Ontario. The PUPS Act was supposed to prohibit these conditions — it has no enforcement mechanism to reach them.

The Auditor General of Ontario launched a formal investigation into Animal Welfare Services in the fall of 2025, prompted by the volume of public complaints about complaints going nowhere. Power was interviewed and supplied them with her spreadsheet of identified mills, along with reference numbers from years of calls into the PAWS Hotline. The investigation is expected to conclude in the fall of 2026. It will also be non-binding. "The Auditor General's review isn't going to be based on policy," Labchuk told me.8 "What they're going to be looking at is whether animal welfare services provide value for money — are they doing what they're supposed to be doing as an enforcement agency." The government that received fifteen years of documented evidence and did nothing is the same government that will decide whether to act on whatever the investigation surfaces. It has no legal obligation to act. It can simply wait.

In June 2024, Doug Ford stood in front of cameras and announced that puppy mills were now prohibited in Ontario. Power had been part of the task force convened for feedback on the PUPS Act. She had told them, along with six other advocacy groups, that without a licensing mechanism, without a registry, the law would be unenforceable. The government thanked them and passed the law anyway. "It's propaganda," she told me. No meat on the bones.13 Labchuk, who testified the same day as the Solicitor General, was equally direct: "There's no legal tool to shut down puppy mills."8

The operations in Wellington County are still there. The barns still hold their inventory. The wire floors are still cold in February. And somewhere between those barns and the families who will eventually surrender the dogs that came out of them, there is a marketplace.

Part III

Scroll. Next Listing.

I want to be honest about something. When I started investigating the online puppy trade, I thought Kijiji would be the easy part. I was wrong.

I spent six weeks in early 2026 — mid-January through late February — documenting puppy advertisements within a 50-kilometre radius of Toronto. Fifty listings. I told myself I was doing it clinically: tracking breeds, prices, postal codes, documentation claims. What I was not prepared for was the feeling that accumulates when you spend six weeks watching the same con run on a rotating cast of breeds, the language barely changing, the emojis reshuffled, the same lies dressed in different dogs' faces.

"⭐️TINY TINY Toy Poodle⭐️." "Exotic Puppies!!" "Family raised, home socialized, vet checked." Scroll. Next listing. Same words, different breed. Scroll. Again. Again.

By week three, I had stopped reading the descriptions and started reading around them — looking for what wasn't there. Of the 50 sellers I documented, only 6 provided any documentation I could independently verify: a real OFA certification number, an actual municipal kennel license, or CKC registration that proved anything about lineage or health testing.14 Six. Twelve percent. The remaining 88% deployed theatrical legitimacy — phrases engineered to produce the feeling of accountability without any of its substance. "Health tested parents." "Champion bloodlines." "European registration," a credential that means nothing in Canada and exists purely to justify a price point four times higher than a shelter adoption fee.5

Worth noting: even a municipal kennel licence or CKC registration doesn't guarantee anything. Mill operators and backyard breeders obtain both — not because they meet welfare standards, but because appearing on the CKC's puppy list is exactly the kind of legitimacy signal that works on buyers who have no way to verify it.35

The municipal kennel licence has its own theatre. Local bylaws cap the number of dogs permitted on a residential property — usually two to four — and anyone exceeding that threshold must apply for a kennel licence, approved by the town council and subject to zoning requirements and annual inspection. These licences exist for trainers and boarding facilities too, which is part of how a mill blends in: the category is broad enough to absorb them. More critically, most municipal bylaws don't specify a maximum number of breeding dogs at all. Which means a licensed operator can have sixty, eighty, a hundred dogs in a barn — fully above board on paper, with a certificate to prove it. And those annual inspections? They are always scheduled weeks in advance. The operators know exactly when the inspector is coming. Sick dogs get moved out. Any animals over a stated limit get temporarily relocated to a friend or neighbour until the appointment is over, then quietly moved back. The filth gets cleaned. The inspector arrives, sees what they were meant to see, and rubber-stamps the renewal. Power has watched it happen. The licence that looks like accountability is just another prop in the same performance — another credential that costs the operation almost nothing and costs the dogs everything.12

One seller in Scarborough was advertising three litters born within ten days — French Bulldogs, Cane Corsos, Pomeranians. Three breeds. Ten days. That is not a family whose dog had an accidental litter. That is a rotation. Multiple females cycling simultaneously, their bodies managed like a production schedule, never given the year between litters that ethical standards require.5 The listing said, "raised with love." I saved it to my folder and moved on because I didn't know what else to do with it.

The breeds are not random. French Bulldogs appeared in 19 of my documented listings. Chihuahuas in 14. Designer mixes — Maltipoos, Cavapoos, Bernedoodles — in another 22, representing 43% of all advertisements I reviewed.14 These are calculated bets on which breeds command the highest price-to-production ratio: dogs small enough to stack in cages, trendy enough to move quickly, built in ways that guarantee expensive medical problems their buyers will only discover later.

88%
of listings had
no verifiable credentials14
43%
designer mixes —
highest margin breeds14
0/19
French Bulldog listings
mentioned BOAS screening14

French Bulldogs cannot breathe properly. That is not a risk — it is a structural feature of the breed. Their skulls are too large for their nasal passages. Their soft palates are elongated. Full corrective BOAS surgery to open their airways runs $3,000 to $5,000.15 Intervertebral disc disease affects French Bulldogs at dramatically elevated rates — a dog can go from walking normally to dragging its hindquarters in forty-eight hours. Total treatment costs run $3,000 to $8,000 or more.6 When it isn't affordable, the dog lives in chronic pain or is euthanized. Not one of the 19 French Bulldog listings I documented mentioned respiratory evaluations.14 Zero of nineteen, for a breed where breathing difficulties and spinal deterioration are near-certainties.

That is how the pipeline works at the point of sale. The buyer isn't being sold a dog. They're being sold a feeling — the belief that the price tag signals quality, that "vet checked" means healthy, that "family raised" means the mother is sleeping on someone's couch instead of standing on a wire floor in Wellington County. It is worth noting that "vet checked" means whatever the seller needs it to mean. There are vets who protect mills. Power has documented one in Brampton — a mobile veterinarian with a complaint file at the College of Veterinarians of Ontario so thick it should have ended his practice. It hasn't.16 What he sells, according to Power, is not veterinary care. It is paperwork: blank, pre-stamped vaccine folders — the official documents that are supposed to be filled in and signed by the vet who administered the vaccines. He sells them blank to mill operators, who fill in the dates themselves, sign them, and hand them to buyers. No vaccines are administered. No vet ever sees the puppy. The folder looks exactly like proof. It isn't. The CVO has received complaint after complaint. It has repeatedly done nothing.16 And the puppies go home without immunity, into households with other animals, into a world full of parvovirus and coccidia and giardia — diseases that tear through unvaccinated litters in close confinement as though the conditions were designed to spread them, which functionally they are. A week later, the puppy is sick. The vet bill for parvo runs $4,000 to $8,000. Many of the puppies don't survive it. The seller, by then, is back behind the listing — new email address, new phone number, same blank folders. Buyers are being sold a story about a dog that does not exist, delivered in the body of a dog whose real story has been carefully hidden from them.

The geography told me the rest. Sixty-eight percent of listings clustered in three postal code zones: Mississauga and Peel Region, Scarborough, and North York.14 Toronto Animal Services reports its highest surrender volumes from the same areas.17 The listing and the surrender intake form are connected by a straight line — eighteen months, sometimes less, the time it takes for a buyer to discover what they actually purchased and run out of road for dealing with it.

I tracked one seller across multiple listings over four weeks. The photos changed. The breed changed — first a Maltipoo, then a Pomeranian, then a litter of Dachshunds with a website called "Dackel Dreams" that mentioned nothing about spinal screening.14 The email address changed. The phone number changed. The writing style didn't. I reported the listings to Kijiji. They were still up the following week.

Kijiji is not screening these sellers. Every listing generates revenue. Every new email address generates a new identity. A single operator can maintain five separate personas simultaneously, each appearing to be a different household.5 Many of the sellers aren't even the mills directly — they're brokers: middlemen who buy entire litters from operators at wholesale, mark them up, and run the sale themselves. The mill stays invisible behind the listing. "It's all about greed," Power told me. "They make money off every ad."13

In 2021, LuAnn Harkness purchased a golden retriever puppy from a Kijiji listing in Markham.30 She named him Rudy. Just over a week later, Rudy was dead from parvovirus. When Harkness called the breeder, they told her it was probably the nerves or the food. When she followed up, the breeders had already sold the house. Their phone was disconnected. They were gone. She had spent $9,000 on Rudy and his medical bills. She got nothing back.30

Rudy's case was not an anomaly. It was the pipeline working exactly as designed.

I kept a running list of phrases across my 50 listings. "Licensed breeder." "Registered." "Vet checked." "Champion bloodlines." By week five, I had stopped being surprised by any of them and started being surprised only when something was absent — when a listing made no claims at all, when it didn't even try. Those listings were easier to read. At least they weren't asking me to trust them. The ones asking me to trust them were the ones that kept me up at night.

And somewhere in the accumulation of those fifty listings — the rotations, the phantom credentials, the Scarborough seller cycling three breeds in ten days — I kept thinking about who was on the other end of each one. Who typed in their credit card number. Who told themselves the $1,400 price tag meant the breeder cared. Who brought a puppy home and called it by a name and didn't know, couldn't have known, what they had just funded. We are the demand that makes the supply profitable. We are the reason the math works. Every purchase from an unverified seller, every listing trusted because the price felt like proof of care, every time someone looked at a puppy and didn't ask where its mother was — we funded the barn. We funded the wire floor. And I spent six weeks watching the front end of that transaction, reading the listings, and I still had to remind myself of it. That the cute photo, the price tag, and the cheerful emoji were the clean room. The barn was somewhere else.

Part IV

Rey

The Toronto Humane Society smells like urine and disinfectant before you're fully through the door. I don't know what I was expecting. I had prepared myself on the way over — told myself I was there to observe, to report, to be professional about it. I started crying before I made it past the second set of doors.

The hallway is smaller than you'd think. A senior Dachshund lay in his bed near the entrance. When I crouched down, he opened his eyes — just enough to acknowledge me — and then looked back at whatever he'd been looking at before I arrived. He didn't get up. He didn't come to the door. I gave him a sad smile and kept on walking. A white pit bull was sitting in her crate — too small for her — and when she saw me, she stood up, walked over and pushed her nose through the gap in the door and nuzzled my hand. Her name, according to the card, was Rey. I told her she was so pretty. She already knew I wasn't taking her home. She nuzzled me anyway.

I walked the hallway a few times, trying to give each dog equal time, and they met me the same way every pass — the brown one with his ears flat and his tongue finding my fingers through the gap, the black one pressing close with the same wide, patient eyes, the same silent question. I knew their names. I didn't know anything else. Not how they got there. Not how long they'd been waiting. The kennel cards told me what they loved. They said nothing about what they'd lost.

Photos: Jahaan Kanji. Toronto Humane Society, February 2026. Dogs waiting in the kennel hallway.
Photos: Jahaan Kanji. Toronto Humane Society, February 2026. LEFT: REY, THE WHITE PIT BULL DESCRIBED IN THIS STORY.

I crouched down in front of Rey before I left. I told her I wished I could bring her home. I meant it, and it didn't help either of us, and I walked back out through the doors that smelled like urine and made my way back home.

The 63% surge in surrenders recorded in 2022 was the moment the scale became impossible to ignore — and it changed almost nothing upstream.17 By the first half of 2025, the Toronto Humane Society was up 28% year-over-year. The Ontario SPCA, province-wide, up 16%.31 These weren't numbers accumulating in spreadsheets. They were living animals whose owners had made calculations that led to the same devastating conclusion: the pet had to go.

Some arrived already broken — French Bulldogs with collapsed airways, Chihuahuas with dental disease so advanced their teeth were loosening, designer mixes whose hip dysplasia was inherited from parents never screened, never tested.33 These dogs flood intake forms citing medical expenses owners cannot afford — a direct line from irresponsible breeding into shelter kennels, predictable as clockwork. But they represent only one stream. The larger, more insidious problem involves perfectly healthy animals whose only misfortune was landing with families who loved them completely — and then hit financial breaking points that made keeping them impossible.37 No collapsed spine. No genetic time bomb. Just a dog who had a home until the system decided its owner didn't deserve one anymore.

The thing nobody puts on the surrender form is the math that preceded it. By 2025, more than half of American pet owners had skipped or declined recommended veterinary care entirely, cost cited as the reason.18 The financial pressure is no less acute in Canada — for households already spending 42% of income on rent in the Greater Toronto Area, a $2,000 emergency vet bill becomes mathematically impossible without sacrificing something else.19 In 2024, cost-related factors accounted for 76 per cent of pet surrenders at Calgary Humane Society — a figure its leadership described as a forced choice, not a voluntary one.31 Between January and November 2023, the Toronto Humane Society enrolled animals in crisis into its Urgent Care program — temporary fostering for families hit by housing instability or financial freefall.9 A peer-reviewed study of the program found that 74% were reunited with their owners once the crisis passed.20 What that figure quietly confirms is something the surrender forms never say out loud: most of these families didn't stop wanting their dog. They hit a wall; the system gave them no way around. The animals didn't need new homes. They needed a bridge. And in the overwhelming majority of cases, when someone built one, they crossed it.

I kept thinking about that number — 74 percent. About what it means: that the crisis was temporary for most of them. That the dog was never the problem. That the problem had an address, and a listing, and a wire floor somewhere west of the city, and nobody in an official capacity had ever been required to visit it.

The dogs arriving at Toronto Animal Services aren't broken. Most of them aren't, anyway. Some are profoundly, expensively, preventably broken, their suffering manufactured upstream and deferred onto families who trusted a listing and a price tag that was supposed to mean something. But the others — the healthy ones, the housebroken ones, the ones who cry in their kennels because they don't understand where their family went34 — are a different kind of casualty. Of a housing market with no room for them. Of a veterinary system priced beyond what working families can access.

She has never been called by a name.

Neither has the dog in the barn in Wellington County, whose puppies will move through a Kijiji listing and into a family and eventually, possibly, into a kennel just like this one. The pipeline that began in a wire cage ends here, behind glass, in a shelter operating beyond capacity — and the distance between those two points, measured in months and money and accumulated grief, is the distance this story has been trying to close.

Faces of the Pipeline

Before & After: Dogs Rescued by Speaking of Dogs

Rescue — Speaking of Dogs Rescue, Ontario
Zack — Before & After
Olive — Before & After
Mandy — Before & After
Photos courtesy of Lorraine Houston / Speaking of Dogs Rescue.
Part V

Behind the No-Kill Curtain

The no-kill movement's headline number — a 90% or higher live-release rate — looks like progress until you ask what's behind it. That designation has become both a genuine achievement and an accounting trick. It reflects real progress while obscuring what happens to the 10% who fall outside the threshold — and how much of what's inside it was simply moved rather than solved.21 More than 11% of shelter animals get transported to other organizations — a practice that counts as a "live release" for the sending facility while relocating the pressure without reducing the population requiring homes.22

This creates a shell game where overcrowded rural shelters transfer animals to urban centres where larger adoption pools give each dog a better chance of finding a home — but this strains urban facilities already absorbing their own intakes, which in turn push overflow to rescue networks on thin margins, which warehouse animals in foster homes that become permanent holding patterns.

When every link hits capacity simultaneously, the pressure has nowhere left to go. In the first quarter of 2024, Ontario shelters recorded 8,359 admissions — 3,281 owner surrenders, 4,503 strays, and 1,575 transfers — a 17% increase over the same period the year before.23 Surrender requests climbed another 17% from 2024 into 2025.36 The system was not stabilizing. It was accelerating.

The dogs least likely to move through that chain are the ones the pipeline sent. The French Bulldog, whose airway was compromised before she was born. The Dachshund, whose spinal disease went unmanaged until the family's finances gave out. They are the pipeline's invoice, deferred, delivered here.

The kennel card says: Great with kids. Needs active family. House-trained. It doesn't say she was never the problem. It doesn't say anything about where the problem actually started — because the kennel card was never designed to hold the weight of how she got there.

That is what the whole system was built to avoid: the question of origin. Where did she come from. Who profited. What was known and ignored and permitted to continue. The card says house-trained. It says great with kids. It says nothing about the barn.

Part VI

The Fix

None of this is hard to solve. That's what makes it so hard to sit with. They've been named in committee testimony and formal letters, and fifteen years of documented evidence. The government has heard them. It has chosen, repeatedly, not to act on them. But it helps to understand what acting would actually look like — not as a list of policy fixes, but as a choice that two Canadian provinces have already made, in opposite directions, and what happened after.

Quebec made its choice in February 2024. Any person keeping more than fifteen dogs must now apply for a provincial license, meet specific care standards, and operate under a hard cap of fifty breeding animals.24,25 Labchuk is the first to say fifty is still too high — but the operative word is cap. There is a number. There is a registry. "Quebec does have a better handle on where these operations are now," she told me, "and some tools to take enforcement if they find them."8 It is an imperfect system. It is also a system which is more than Ontario has.

Manitoba made a different choice. Manitoba actually has a licensing regime for dog breeders on the books — in the legislation, in the regulations, written down and passed and technically in effect. "The province has just chosen never to enforce or practice carrying out that regulation," Labchuk told me, "and unfortunately, it's incredibly difficult to challenge those decisions."8 The law exists. The mechanisms of enforcement were never built. The breeders operate as if it doesn't, because for practical purposes it doesn't. Ontario is currently writing the same version of this story — and Manitoba is the preview of where that story goes if no one forces a different ending.

Quebec on one side. Manitoba, on the other. Ontario standing between them, in possession of a law with no teeth and a Solicitor General who cannot tell you how many puppy mills are operating in his own province.

The fix, as Labchuk described it, is not ambitious. It is administrative. What the province needs to do, she said, is know who these people are — where the dogs are being kept, and be able to go and do an inspection, and have standards to inspect against. That starts with licensing. Every breeder registered, every operation assigned a number, that number required on every listing and every sale, so that a buyer can verify in thirty seconds that the person selling them a dog is who they claim to be. The cap on breeding animals needs to be low — not Quebec's fifty, which Labchuk calls too high, but a few dogs per operation, small enough that one inspector arriving unannounced could actually look each animal in the eye. And the welfare standards need to be written in language that means something: degrees Celsius, square footage, documented veterinary schedules.

"Right now, dogs in Ontario breeding operations are not entitled to any fresh air, any sunlight, any exercise. They're not entitled to any specific socialization, which, for a very social animal like a dog, is a horrible thing to contemplate."

Camille Labchuk — Executive Director, Animal Justice

Those are not descriptions of gaps in the law. They are descriptions of things the law currently permits. The registries buyers trust to signal quality — both the Canadian Kennel Club and its American imitator, the Continental Kennel Club — offer no protection either. Neither screens for welfare. Neither investigates conditions. As Labchuk puts it: "The CKC exists to protect the interests of breeders, not the interests of dogs."35

Require registration numbers on online listings — or remove the listings entirely. A petition demanding Kijiji ban all animal sales has collected hundreds of thousands of signatures.26 Meanwhile, a convicted animal abuser in Nova Scotia was caught running sales through her daughter's account after a lifetime ban, because creating a new email address takes thirty seconds.27 The platform profits from every listing. It has no incentive to build the verification infrastructure that would reduce that volume. Which means the government has to force the question — the same way Quebec has built a searchable public database of licensed breeders that a buyer can check before typing in their credit card number.28 Ontario has no such database, because Ontario has no such registry, because Ontario has a law that was passed without the mechanism that would make it mean anything.

The infrastructure to prevent this already exists — it just isn't being funded. In 2024, the Ontario SPCA performed 9,276 spay/neuter surgeries, preventing an estimated 539,120 potential offspring from entering a system already beyond capacity.29 Registration spots fill within hours of opening. The OSPCA submitted a proposal to the 2025 provincial budget requesting a grant program to expand veterinary access in underserved communities. It hasn't been funded.

The barriers are real. Political will is the first and largest. There is no legal mechanism to force the government's hand on the PUPS Act's outstanding regulations. They can defer indefinitely, and they have.12 The OSPCA wrote to the Premier.32 The letter was received. The regulations are still unwritten. And as Labchuk said — careful, precise, a lawyer who has been in these rooms and knows exactly what she's describing — "it's a common thing we see when governments introduce legislation that really depends on enacting regulations to give it teeth. We've often seen governments just never do that."8 Not fail to do it. Never do it. The distinction matters.

The dog in the kennel was surrendered on a Tuesday. She came in with a leash, a bag of food, and a family who cried the whole drive over. She has been here for thirty-eight days. She is healthy. She is housebroken. She is waiting.

There is another one — found at two in the morning in a park in Scarborough, hiding in a bush, shaking. Nobody claimed him. He may have a family somewhere — a leash that fit him, a name they used to call him, a couch he slept on before whatever happened that made leaving him in a park seem like the only option. Nobody knows. He can't say. He is also waiting.

Neither of them is waiting for a licensing registry or a measurable welfare standard or a mandatory registration field on a Kijiji listing. They are waiting for a person to stop walking and choose them.

The system that put them there is still running. The government knows how to stop it. It has been told, repeatedly, in committee testimony and formal letters and fifteen years of documented evidence, exactly what to do.

It just hasn't cared enough to act.

But somewhere west of the city, past the subdivisions and the box stores and the county roads where the buggies still run, there is a barn. And in that barn, there is a wire cage. And in that cage, there is a dog who is four years old and whose body is beginning to show what four years of this costs. She doesn't know she is being evaluated. All she knows is that the mornings feel harder lately and the wire floor holds the cold like it was built to.

She has never been called by a name. She is still there.
